As a workaround, I pre-saved all my sound lengths to CSV using the `pydub` library.  All my sounds are .ogg so you'll have to change this if this is not the case for you.

```
import os, csv
from glob import glob

from pydub import AudioSegment

from flatten import flatten

cur_dir = os.path.dirname(__file__)
media_dir = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(cur_dir), 'media')
sound_paths = (glob(os.path.join(x[0], '*.ogg')) for x in os.walk(media_dir))
# Flatten it, since the lists might contain other lists
south_paths_flattened = list(flatten(sound_paths))

with open('sound_lengths.csv', 'w') as csvfile:
    csvwriter = csv.writer(csvfile, delimiter=',', lineterminator='\n')

    for sound_path in south_paths_flattened:
        print("sound_path: %s" % sound_path)
        try:
            sound = AudioSegment.from_ogg(sound_path)
        except Exception:
            print("Could not load file.")
        else:
            csvwriter.writerow([os.path.normpath(sound_path),
                                str(len(sound) / 1000.)])
```

Then I load sounds using the static method `load` of `LankiSoundLoader`, just the same as if I was going to load the sound with `SoundLoader.load`.

("Lanki" is just a made-up word I created.)

```
class LankiSoundLoader(object):
    """
    A custom factory class that substitutes for SoundLoader

    """
    sound_lengths = {}

    @staticmethod
    def load_sound_lengths():
        with open('sound_lengths.csv', 'r') as csvfile:
            csvreader = csv.reader(csvfile, delimiter=',')
            for row_number, row in enumerate(csvreader):
                print(repr(row))
                LankiSoundLoader.sound_lengths[row[0]] = float(row[1])

    @staticmethod
    def load(sound_file_path):
        sound_file_path = os.path.normpath(sound_file_path)
        
        if not LankiSoundLoader.sound_lengths:
            LankiSoundLoader.load_sound_lengths()

        sound = SoundLoader.load(sound_file_path)
        
        lanki_sound = sound
        try:
            sound_length = LankiSoundLoader.sound_lengths[sound_file_path]
        except KeyError:
            # If we don't have this sound on record in the CSV, hope
            # that the length mthod actually words
            pass
        else:
            # It's actually the _get_length method we must overrride, as per
            # https://github.com/kivy/kivy/blob/master/
            #                          kivy/core/audio/__init__.py
            lanki_sound._get_length = lambda : sound_length
        
        return lanki_sound
```

> He calls it "binning", which is the same as nominal categorization. But more importantly, he compares the different value ranges for best gain in info probability. This is the central reason for categorical encoding in statistical inference. The coding reasons in statistics are just a convenience, but the real reason is finding info gain. 

Here it sounds like you're talking about the problem of, given a continuous random variable, find a discretization that preserves the most information (I guess under the constraint that each discrete value has to correspond to a contiguous range of the continuous space)? Patsy's categorical encodings are about taking a categorical variable, and encoding it as a multi-dimensional real vector. I'm not sure what connection you see between these two problems, or whether you mean something else.

>  - Wikipedia article <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_(information_theory)#Entropy_as_a_measure_of_diversity>, see the b-ary section

The b-ary section just gives the definition of the entropy of a categorical random variable (with `b` setting the base of the log). I know what entropy is; what I don't know is what it has to do with categorical encodings :-).

> For implementing in patsy, I envsion one or more categorical variables fed via the "formula" along with a target variable that is a continuous variable. This target var is what gets the ranges or bins (as in the bins parameter for np.dititize). Patsy will then calculate max "information gain" figure (which is a probability value between 0 and 1) for the categories based on a vareity of ranges of the continuous variable. Instead of zeros and 1s, it will use the floating point values for each of the categories.

If you're using a 'user' build, you will not be able to use the Android repo SL4A at all, in which case it might be better to skip SL4A entirely. To do so, in config:
```
"AndroidDevice": [
  {"serial": <serial>, "skip_sl4a": true}
]
```

Without SL4A, you can still control the android device with adb calls and [Mobly Snippets](https://github.com/google/mobly-snippet-lib). (P.S. We are working on a general snippet implementation to cover basic Android operations.)

The focus of this fetch spec is indeed on browser implementation, but the concept is not supposed to be irrelevant for other clients.

About publishing dictionaries: indeed the client does not tell the server which it has, instead the server provides the URL of the dictionary, and the client caches it. This is good when the client then requests other pages which use the same dictionary.
When using a previous document as dictionary for a next document, the server should know that the client already has this previous document since it sent it before during this session. Does the latter sound feasible?

About recursive-sbr: the dictionary itself can be compressed with for example `br`. This is to prevent dictionaries fetching other dictionaries fetching other dictionaries and so on. Good point about updating dictionary from other dictionary though. Maybe it makes more sense to allow some recursion but limit the depth.

Giving an update here, since this might be of more general interest.  (Erin and I have been discussing this offline.)

@esheldon discovered a problem when he converted the test script to match what he really does in metacal, where he computes a pixel-free PSF, dilates that for the target PSF, and then convolves by the real pixel again.

When we run this version with a fairly complex WCS, the noise in the image blows up.  Like variance = 1.e6, rather than <1 in the previous version.  It only happened if the WCS is complex enough.  For things with ~squarish pixels, there was no problem.  But above a certain threshold, the noise would suddenly explode.

I've tracked down the problem to small values being inverted by SBDeconvolve.  The values were small, but not precisely zero, and then because the target PSF gets expanded by (1+2g), the small kvalues in the original didn't precisely match the places where the target PSF is near zero.  So it doesn't get nulled out in the product.

My solution (993044f1eb13d577e27d6f48fed23b5059ed8387), which seems to work is to have SBDeconvolve calculate a minimum value to trust based on the GSParams kvalue_accuracy (= 1.e-5 by default).  min_acc_kval = kvalue_accuracy * flux_adaptee  Then anything closer to zero than this doesn't go to 1/kval, but rather goes to 1/min_acc_kval.

I think this is valid, since these k-values are actually uncertain at the level of min_acc_kval, so we don't actually know it to better than this.  It seems appropriate to use the minimum allowed inverse rather than let it produce values of ~1.e9 that you can get by just doing 1/kval.

@perzizzle Yes, this will be covered in the updated README as well. The different sources will be merged if there is more than 1. You can use the flag multiple times, and the final one will take priority. All of them take priority over the job template extra-vars. So your command could include --extra-vars="a=1 b=2" --extra-vars="a=3", and you would end up with a=3 and b=2. You can include a file as in --extra-vars="@filename.yml", but it's priority will be set by its order just like all the other flags.

What matters most is that the job template variables are over-written, and that they're not forgotten when even more variables are passed. It will do that correctly.

Hi,

I looked at your work and I have a question. Why did you choose to model this feature as two new types of fields, whereas in ClickHouse these are not fields but options that can be applied to each field?

What I mean is, why not allow something like this:
username = StringField(alias='name')
or:
mat_int = Int32Field(materialized='abs(int_field)')

Field classes are mapped to datatypes (e.g. string field, int field etc.) both in ClickHouse and infi.clickhouse_orm. Alias and materialized are not datatypes, but rather field options (just like default value). So it's better to model them as such.

The transact decorator
======================

A @transact decorator was added that allows to write continuations for transcation
instructions. The code that controls what happens before and after a transaction 
can now be written in each specific instruction

```
    @transact
    @concretized_args(in_offset='SAMPLED', in_size='SAMPLED')
    def CALL(self, gas, address, value, in_offset, in_size, out_offset, out_size):
        '''Message-call into an account'''
        self.world.start_transaction('CALL',
                                     address,
                                     data=self.read_buffer(in_offset, in_size),
                                     caller=self.address,
                                     value=value,
                                     gas=self.gas)
        raise StartTx()

    @CALL.pos
    def CALL(self, gas, address, value, in_offset, in_size, out_offset, out_size):
        data = self.world.current_transaction.return_data

        if data is not None:
            data_size = len(data)
            size = Operators.ITEBV(256, Operators.ULT(out_size, data_size), out_size, data_size)
            self.current_vm.write_buffer(out_offset, data[:size])

        return self.world.last_transaction.return_value
```

Legacy symbolic memory indexing approach.
=========================================

In Linux/Decree symbolic indexes used to access memory are eagerly solved. When a symbolic index is used to access memory all the solutions for the index are calculated on the spot. The resultant symbolic expression is then built over the solutions of the index using mostly the operator ITE. For example a symbolic `s_index` that has 2 solutions: 0x0000 and 0x0001. And the actual memory contents id "AB" then the expression of reading memory at index s_index is something like this:

    ITE(s_index==0, 'A', ITE( s_index==1, 'B', '\x00))
 
Knowing that s_index can not be solved by other values than {0, 1} then the default option is never used.
In practice the resultant expresion is smaller easier to solve than the Array alternative. Though that technique can only be applied if the number of potential solutions for the index is reliativelly small. Historically having a long set of solutions (> 0x1000) for a memory access would be treated as a finding in itself and something that requieres human inspection.


Using raw smtlib array to model memory.
=======================================

In this approach memory and storage (and transaction call data and result) will translate almost direclty to an smtlib array. In smtlib you have arrays that map bitvectors of any size to bitvectors of any size.

```
(declare-fun MEMORY () (Array (_ BitVec 256) (_ BitVec 8)))
(declare-fun STORAGE () (Array (_ BitVec 256) (_ BitVec 256)))
(declare-fun tx1_data () (Array (_ BitVec 256) (_ BitVec 8)))
```

String a value to a symbolic smtlib array builds a new expression.

```
MEMORY1 := (store MEMORY INDEX VALUE)
```

So we need to keep the last version of the expression representing the memory and all the other arrays (calldata, returndata, storage)

In this case reading an smtlib array offset that was not written before returns a free symbolic bitvector. In EVM reading from an unused slot will return a zero. So some housekeeping is needed. We need to maintain the list of used slots and on read build an expression that uses the correct default.

Example
=======
In Ethereum, accesing memory and storage with free symbolic index is more much common op. Consider this CTF challenge from https://capturetheether.com/challenges/math/mapping/

The goal is to write a `true` value (!=0) to `isComplete` storage variable (slot zero). -Spoiler follows- For this challenge you need to send two transactions. In the first tx you set the array size to 0xfff..ffff effectivelly uncapping the array `map`. In the second tx as the array `map` is unlimited you can write to any storage offset.

```
source_code = '''pragma solidity ^0.4.21;

contract MappingChallenge {
    bool public isComplete;
    uint256[] map;

    function set(uint256 key, uint256 value) public payable {
        // Expand dynamic array as needed
        if (map.length <= key) {
            map.length = key + 1;
        }

        map[key] = value;
    }
}
'''
```

There is an obscure offset calculation in place to actually writting to isComplete 
by using a `map` offset. The idea is to write 1 to a free symbolic index and let 
manticore calculate the right offsets. The script looks like this:

```
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM, Detector

m = ManticoreEVM()
source_code = ''' ... '''
print "Source code:\n",source_code

user_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)
contract_account = m.solidity_create_contract(source_code, owner=user_account)

contract_account.set(m.SValue,1)
contract_account.set(m.SValue,1)

for st in m.all_states:
    flag_storage_slot = 0
    flag_value = st.platform.get_storage_data(contract_account.address, flag_storage_slot)
    if st.can_be_true(flag_value != 0):
        print "Flag Found! Check ", m.workspace
        st.constraints.add(flag_value != 0)
        m.generate_testcase(st, 'Flag Found', '')

```
This simply sends 2 symbolic tx and ten search among the resultant states for one in which the `isComplete` _could_ be 1. If found it generates a testcase for it

Sorry, let me elaborate: the standard use case would be when you want to do greedy search or sampling, which could be done like this:

    x = start_of_sentence
    while x != end_of_sentence:
      rnn_state = rnn_state.update(lookup(x))
      log_prob = make_prediction(rnn_state)
      x = argmax(log_prob.value())

Currently in order to do something like this, you need to call `.npvalue()` (in Python) or `as_vector()` in C++, then do the argmax over that in the native language. The main reason why this is bad is particularly on GPU, where it requires that memory be transferred to the CPU and then back again to do the lookup, and can't take advantage of the fact that GPUs will plow through an argmax much faster than the CPU.

In this commit I still haven't implemented `lookup(x)` where `x` is an IndexTensor, but that will be forthcoming.

Having the many small .cu files compiled independently has significantly increased the compile time of gdynet on MSVC (from 4.5 minutes for a single monolithic .cu file to 18 minutes for the current split of 30 files), because MSVC (unfortunately) doesn't do parallel building of that part of the compilation (they are custom actions calling nvcc, rather than using the built-in compiler). 

I consider that MSVC's fault. However, note there is a lot of overhead in compiling each .cu file because the majority of the code is Eigen that is getting parsed and compiled over and over. That 4x increase in compile time is comparing two sequential compiles, so it shows how much overhead there is. I would imagine this overhead is also hurting the parallel Linux build, and that it would probably be faster to build in Linux if there were just, say, 4-8 files built in parallel instead of the current 30. This would also have the side-benefit of speeding up the MSVC compile.

I would think it's useful to write out a FITS WCS (ie. option 2 or 3) as you propose above, but *also* encode the full representation *somehow* (it can be more obscure), so that you can read the data back in from the FITS file without loss of information. Of course, this may raise the painful issue of representations getting out of sync, but it solves a couple of practical problems (including visualization with existing tools) and would work as long as you don't do anything unusual... Off the top of my head, you could include a "WCS checksum" and refuse to re-load the "full" WCS encoding if the FITS version has been user-modified; if that's not good enough, the user has to make their modifications with `gwcs` instead of editing the FITS headers (which re-generates the checksum). Or you could perhaps declare the FITS representation read-only and warn people not to mess with it.

Maybe this is more complexity than you want, but I think it would be nice to have. It might be tough to persuade Gemini to use ASDF in the near future, so we'll need some way to serialize `gwcs` in FITS if we use it in the meantime. I probably need to make a bit more progress on a little proof-of-concept using `gwcs` that I started earlier in the year before working on I/O, but it's something I might be able to help with if this is still pending, say, a few months from now.

Not really, since I'm not the WCS expert. But I guess I contribute this much: given that the IFU cubes are constructed on a regular, linearized frame, their WCS should be pretty simple to document and fully describable by the traditional FITS standard WCS keywords (e.g. crpix, crval, cdelt, etc.), which as far as I know is what's contained in model.meta.wcsinfo. So documenting the cube's WCS in the wcsinfo object is valid, but I agree with Mihai that it should also always be documented in the "official" (gwcs-based) WCS object contained in model.meta.wcs. In the case of a nice regular frame like the cubes have, the results from both should be identical. But the point of the gwcs-based wcs object is that it also provides methods that allow for easy transformation back and forth between pixel and sky frames, which is what Mihai apparently needs. I don't believe the simple wcsinfo object allows for that.

> So now since you removed Copy trait from Value struct, does that mean now when you assign (bind) the return value to a variable, the ownership is transferred as the consequence of the assignment? And previously, the struct was just copied field by field?

@JieGhost: No: rust has move semantics by default, so the vast majority of the time, the value (and its ownership) were _moved_ from one owner to another. Copy is used implicitly in positions where the borrow checker determines that two paths will end up using the same struct. A lot of the changes (like the one you pointed out above) were positions where we were implicitly (maybe even unnecessarily) copying this data. But I think it was still fine to have marked Value `Copy`, because Value is only ~16 bytes.

Update: I made a dummy version of JavacInfo that just throws an informative exception in all methods (so we can be really sure it's the one being called). 

 If we put that on the bootclasspath then Zinc does indeed use it.

If we put it on the regular classpath then Zinc still uses it. This is because when `getSystemJavaCompiler()` does the "falls back to finding the compiler that comes with the current runtime" thing, it finds tools.jar, wraps it in a URLClassLoader and uses that to load the class. But remember that classloaders delegate to their parent before attempting to load the class themselves. And this URLClassLoader's parent is the regular classloader, which loads from our regular classpath.  So that explains it.

Conclusions:

A) If we want to use a custom javac, we just have to put it on the classpath instead of our Java Distribution's tools.jar ("it" means something with a `com.sun.tools.javac.api.JavacTool`).

B) If we want to use the current distribution's tools.jar, we don't need to explicitly find it and put it on the classpath at all.  `getSystemJavaCompiler()` does that for us.

C) If we want to use error prone then we need to either create a custom `com.sun.tools.javac.api.JavacTool` that will run the custom error prone entry point, or modify Zinc to find Javac in some way other than via `getSystemJavaCompiler()`. The former is probably easier.

OK, so two separate things need to happen:

1. zinc_compile.py needs an option to set a custom javac_classpath. I have a change in the works for this.  This is all that is needed in order to, e.g., run javac 9 on JDK 8.

2. To use compiler entry point with a name other than `com.sun.tools.javac.api.JavacTool` (e.g., error prone requires this) then we'll need to modify zinc to optionally do so.  This wouldn't be difficult.

 Note that error prone delegates actual compilation to `com.sun.tools.javac.api.JavacTool`, so we can't attempt to be clever by creating our own `com.sun.tools.javac.api.JavacTool` which delegates to error prone - we'd end up with an infinite recursion.

The issue is that PyDrive is trying to load the entire file into memory before uploading, which is very inefficient for larger files.  It's a bug with PyDrive, presumably, since it's claims the Upload() method picks the most efficient way to send the file.

Resumable, chunked uploads are supported by the underlying client, though you'd need to 
You can look at the Python code samples for the alternate way of doing with the apiclient:
https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/reference/files/insert

You can also change the chunk size of the upload by setting it in the parameters of the MediaFileUpload, like so:
media_body = MediaFileUpload(file_path, mimetype=mime_type, chunksize=1024*1024, resumable=True)

Yeah, it's the client-side logic that is slow. It is actually O(n^2) when batch-adding a bunch of exercises because here's the logic:

In the `addExercise` action

1. For each exercise E chosen to be added
1. For each exercise {F} already chosen
1. If E is in {F}, then add, else don't

Because {F} is growing at every time a new E is added, the loop in `addExercise` gets longer with each new E.

Maybe the fix is to right a specialization of `addExercises` that considers all the new Es at once.

For context @texasaggie97  and I talked about some pros / cons of this
- For read/fetch functions
  - User is restricted on how they can modify the data (can't append or delete elements).  We both felt this wasn't too bad
  - I wish there was a good way to document / enforce that array vs list vs custom type is an implementation detail and we can change it later.  In theory, people could do type checks or use the array-specific functions and we'd break them by changing
- For write functions
  - I'd be concerned about the usability.  If the usability is bad, then what are we gaining by offering it?  They might as well use numpy which interops with their existing numpy code.
  - Would read/fetch use memory views or arrays?  What about write?  Would an inconsistency be a problem?
  - If we just don't offer this for write, would the inconsistency with read be a problem?

Note on inconsistency: this mostly applies to APIs that have both input/output like XNET and DAQmx.  How much consistency do we prefer within nimi-python

Ok, @pdasigi, this is ready to look at.  Sorry it's such a monstrosity at this point.  I think it's a whole lot cleaner than it was before, though.  The main points:

1. I removed batching from the action selection computation.  This dramatically simplifies the logic, because there's no more global action id stuff, and it lets us remove a whole lot of code.  As part of this, I made the `GrammarState` hold onto more information about each action, because it was more convenient to keep in inside the `valid_actions` dictionary itself.  You can see what this looks like by looking at the test for `GrammarState`.
2. I combined the `State` and `Step` classes between both semantic parsers, because they were basically just duplicates of each other.  Now we have general classes that can be re-used across any semantic parsing model.
3. I renamed the merged `State` and `Step` classes to reflect what they actually do, and split them out into a few related classes.  Instead of `WikiTablesDecoderState`, we now have `GrammarBasedDecoderState` and `CoverageDecoderState`, where the `CoverageDecoderState` is a subclass that just adds a `ChecklistState` object.  Similarly, we have a `BasicTransitionFunction` (that's an LSTM decoder with attention), and a `LinkingTransitionFunction`, `CoverageTransitionFunction` and `LinkingCoverageTransitionFunction`, that all build on this.  The NLVR MML model just uses the `BasicTransitionFunction`, the WikiTables MML model uses the `LinkingTransitionFunction`, and the ERM models add coverage to their respective transition functions.  Most of the functionality stays in the `BasicTransitionFunction` class, though, which is nice.
4. I also cleaned up a bunch of the interface between the `SemanticParser` models and their MML and ERM subclasses.

I'm still getting a few test failures on CI that don't show up locally, and I have some mypy stuff to figure out.  But those should be small fixes, and so the code is ready to review now.

In Azure, all your boxes should be behind the same subdomain. In your case myapp.cloudapp.net. They're part of a cluster and all get private IP addresses that are routable between the virtual machines.

After that you need to use the public IP address for your cluster to setup your DNS entries that are part of that cluster. You can see what I mean in the video below where I highlight the path to find that info:

![Azure Cluster IP](http://g.recordit.co/6suOqj25Vx.gif)

Your DNS setup should then use an A record to that IP with a CNAME for *.mydomain.com so all apps point to it. 

Your register setup looks correct, so I think your biggest issue is that you're hosting each VM under it's own cloudapp.net subdomain right now instead of under the same <mysub>.cloudapp.net.

Ok.  So one issue cropped up in testing.  In comparing the performance of the new logger implementation to the old, I've determined that the major bottleneck for both is the drain.  In testing this, I've sent many (millions?) of message to my free Papertrail account today.  At some point, it would appear they started throttling me.  This actually exposed an issue...

If a write to the drain blocks until timeout (because of a problem on the other end), the logger's internal queue can get backed up.  That means messages sit around waiting to be stored and drained since this happens sequentially to preserve the order of messages.  If that queue fills up completely, the logger starts dropping new messages since there's no vacancy in the queue.  Bottom line... if the drain starts failing, you're also not storing messages.  Yikes.

(For what it's worth, the old logger implementation panics when this happens.  The unit restarts the logger and you start with an empty queue again, but eventually get right back into the same scenario.  Bottom line is the old implementation flaps when this happens.)

I spoke with @technosophos and we agreed that a good strategy here would be to use two separate queues for storage and drainage.  Additionally, since the drainage queue is the one that's more likely to back up-- especially if there are problems on the other end of the drain, we can make the timeouts on the drain writes inversely proportionate to the queue's current depth... is your queue nearly empty?  Take your time sending the message.  Is the queue nearly full or overflowing?  Move things along faster-- even if it means some writes fail.  This way newly arriving messages aren't as likely to get dropped due to a queue full of messages that probably can't be drained anyway.

I think the problem with each of these is that a user that doesn't know to store credentials in an encrypted (home) directory won't see the point of (or at least won't go looking for) these solutions. Unless you're suggesting making one of these the default.

We should probably make it clear in the docs that PaginatedResources are designed to make several lazy network calls and handle potentially very large amounts of response data, and thus don't record their results. Putting it in a NOTE: might get people's attention.

However, I feel like many users don't read the docs until they run into a problem, so I am very in favor of option 3. We should at the very least let people know they are doing something wrong if they are looking for more results at the end of a PaginatedResource.

Adding options 1 and/or 2 would be nice, but might be misused. Would need notes about proper usage.
If we do both, calling reset with `store_results=True` should raise an error.

Not a huge fan of 4, would rather make the user call `reset()` than make it easy for them to use inefficient behavior, lest we get complaints about the "SDK being slow" or something.

While investigating this issue in signed messages, I also looked into transactions. What I found is that web3js v1 0-pads `r` and `s` values in signed transactions. But things started to get hairy when implementing that and I got suspicious. So I looked at geth and parity. Neither of them 0-pad their r and s values in the raw transaction.

Interestingly, that means that exactly the same transaction is created with different hashes in geth/parity vs in web3js. I imagine that clients will import with or without leading 0s just fine. But perhaps this is a surprise to some.

However, signed messages in web3.js, geth, and the `eth_keys` library all produce zero-padded 65-byte signatures. That seems like an obvious thing to fix.

So I believe the conclusion is this:

- Zero-pad the `r` and `s` values when represented as hex inside a combined "signature bytes" value
- Continue to *not* zero-pad `r` and `s` values in transactions (consistent with geth/parity, inconsistent with the web3.js)
- When returned as individual values, `r` and `s` should be returned as integers instead of bytes, to avoid confusion and better match the underlying representation

> I'm not sure this is true. I definitely want python 3.6.4 instead of python 3.6.1. I definitely want the latest openssl.

Honestly, I'm not as worried about details like this. Packages that are properly designed are not going to be pinned to x.x.x releases of a piece of software _unless there is good reason for them to be_.

So for instance, supposed I have a package `foo` and version 1.0 is tied to `python 3.6`, and version 2.0 is tied to `python 3.6.1`. Let's assume the package maintainer knows what he's doing here---there was a reason why it was important to pin to that version of Python. Well, If I do `conda install foo`, I _expect_ Python to get pinned to 3.6.1 as part of that transaction. I do not want it deciding that I should accept `foo 1.0` just so I can get the latest version of Python.

So that's just a 2-level version optimization consideration. But I think the thinking maps to multiple levels quite nicely.

As a workaround, for conda 4.3.x (the current latest in anaconda default channels) we could modify [install.py](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.x/conda/cli/install.py) in conda.cli at the line where action_set is created by calling install_actions_list (currently line 257). Yeah, the problem really is the different conventions that conda and conda-forge adopts. There's frankly no way that our nomkl flag can affect how conda-forge's packages are selected, because they don't use our feature sets.

I'm not sure if the Anaconda folks are going to be keen to rework their MKL/nomkl conventions. That's going to require a fair amount of metadata hotfixing.

Comments welcome!

A strawman proposal: 
Each line in the info/files metadata file can be a scalar as it is now or a (src,dest) tuple. If a tuple, conda links src to dest. Soft links are preferred on unix to avoid having copies when across filesystems.

Pros: fully backward-compatible, straightforward to implement, allows mix of system files with packaged files (maybe not a pro because I have no use-case for it and don't know if it is even wise)
Cons: versioning checking is probably needed (do the files found at the location given actually match the metadata?)  Can we use cksum to check that the files referenced have not been changed from expectations?

Open questions:
How would conda-build create such a package?
- Conda-build could take command-line metadata to create the package and post it to a local channel. It could be similar to the conda metapackage command, but a file with (src,dest) also included along with a local directory in which to create (or update) the local channel. Or, there could be a new "source" key in meta.yaml that has a list of tuples that are the files. Something like:
source:
    - system:
        - src, dest
        - src2, dest2
src is a full path while dest is relative to the python env root.

@swevrywhere Your issue is not related to this thread.  I moved it to https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6292

I want to disambiguate between two different types of "optional dependencies."  

The type most here might be familiar with are the pip type, denoted by square brackets.  Let's call those "aliased group dependencies."  The single alias refers to a group of additional dependencies.

The second type I'd like to call "conditional dependencies."  This type in fact is completely different and orthogonal to the first.  This type solves the problem of why the conda-forge `django` package has a dependency on `gdal`.  This type tells the conda solver, for example, *if `gdal` is going to be installed in this environment it must conform to a certain version specification.  The conda-forge `django` package would thus ideally declare this conditional dependency in it's meta.yaml.

Two separate types of optional dependencies.  I fully support the latter, and it's on the conda roadmap.  (I know...  I just need to clean it up so I can make it public.)  The first type I'm not completely onboard with yet.  With the second type properly implemented, users are free to add whatever additional dependencies they want, while the package maintainer is still guaranteed the ability to control the versions of *optional* dependencies that end up in the environment.

conda config --set path_conflict prevent

This was built into conda 4.3.  The other settings for `path_conflict` are `warn` and `clobber`.  The default in conda 4.3 was intended to be `prevent`, but there are just too many conflicts right now and it would be been visibly painful to users to roll out without cleaning up our repositories first.

In future conda feature releases we're going to try to transition the default setting to `warn` and then finally to `prevent`.

This is an interesting feature request.  Unfortunately, it's not as simple as merely "merging" indices.  It involves a full reindexing of all indices into another index.

This is not a lightweight operation.  It potentially could be very taxing to clusters, especially if you're already concerned about memory consumption.

You'd consume double the space on your cluster by the time the re-index completed (or more, depending on the number of replicas).

There are many other issues that could arise from this.  I'll ponder on it.

I was waiting for others to comment, I've already voiced my thoughts elsewhere but I'll reiterate them here.

I don't think this is a good idea for a few reasons:

- It's against the idea of sharing configurations with other individuals.  A definition of a hook should live in one place and consumers should references that single place
- It doesn't work for multiple repositories (two of these "phantom" repositories would reference the same hooks.yaml which would introduce available but broken hook ids to end users)
- Installation of these "phantom" hook repositories would be orphaned from their configuration, potentially leading to unexpected behaviour when using pre-commit across multiple repositories on the same machine
- It introduces yet-another-way to do things which would require maintenance

On the note of a central repository of hooks:
- One already exists: http://pre-commit.com/hooks.html or in consumable format http://pre-commit.com/all-hooks.json
- However, depending on this in a runtime situation would introduce a single-point-of-failure and would require organizations which want to use pre-commit to set up an internal "pre-commit repository" (this is a huge barrier to entry for many, especially enterprise).

On the note of non-VCS:
- This again goes against the direction of reusable hook definitions, but it also introduces significant logic into pre-commit to understand each and every package management system and the quirks of each of them

I do not like the idea of having a Client that passively tries to open TCP connections to keep the pool in a certain size. Here who has the last call is the server and the user, if the server supports keep alive, the connection will keep it for a while, once its internal timeout is reached the connection is closed. If the user makes an HTTP request and there is no connections available and opened the Client will open a new connection. This is the normal situation for most of the cases.

Indeed this is more an INFX issue than a software issue, long life connections in low latency networks that do not have sudden changes in their topology are a good idea - AWS Load Balancers drops of all connections that are not being used after 60 seconds with the service that is being proxied. But for other networks, such as the mobile ones this might be a bad idea and a source of other problems that are not easy to solve or need another protocol.

ELB is their old tcp balancer, ALB is the new http load balancer

Here's a scenario to make this clearer:

We have a simple server running behind the load balancer, the keep_alive timeout is set to the default 75s, a client sends an http request to the load balancer every second, the load balancer is trying to improve performance by maintaining a long-lived connection to the backend using the keep-alive/connection header.

The connection is never idle for more than a second, and yet once every 75s our aiohttp server will decide to hard reset the connection even if the client is in the process of using it. This guarantees that clients will see flaky behavior from the server.

A better implementation would perhaps also include a max number of requests to handle, at which point the final request could send back the appropriate header which would cause the load balancer to perform a clean disconnect/reconnect without the client being impacted.

Hi Andrew,
Great work by the way - don't mean to complain, the case I had is below. 
I used python 3's urllib to quote a json snippet passed as a GET argument.  urllib encodes the ":" as %3A, the partially encoded url is then used to "sign" the request by adding an extra header.

Yarl inside aiohttp was then reversing the encoding of %3A back to ":" before sending the request, so the api-signature header no longer matched the request. It seems the server checks the signature *before* against the urlencoded value.

From what I have learned subsequently, colon is a permissible character in URLs, so it is actually urllib that is at fault here for encoding it. 

```
	url = 'https://server.com/.../blah?filter=' + urllib.parse.quote('{"open": true}')
	...
	headers = {}
	headers['api-nonce'] = nonce
	headers['api-key'] = apiKey
	headers['api-signature'] = generate_rest_signature(apiSecret, method, url, nonce, body_text)

	async with session.request(method, url, headers=headers, data=body) as resp:
		...
```

I guess the question is:

1. should the user be able to specify what URL should be used to connect to the notebook server in general (connection_url), or
2. *only* specify what URL should be printed in the logs (display_url), i.e. what a human should use to open the notebook, but not affect anything that discovers the URL programmatically.

1 would allow things like `jupyter notebook list` to provide useful info for connecting from outside the container, but possibly break things like `jupyter notebook stop` *inside* the container. 2 suggests that we never want the notebook to know its own URL that things should use, e.g. reporting its own connection URL for use outside the container after the initial log.

From your use cases, I think `jupyter notebook list` in the container would work *better* in the first case, because it would give you the info you need to connect from outside the container, rather than having to dig into the logs for that. That said, I don't think that the process-management tools like `notebook list` and `notebook stop` make a great deal of sense in a container, since there's necessarily only one server and it can be terminated normally with `docker stop` commands, etc.

I genuinely don't know the right answer for this. Overriding display_url is certainly the least disruptive since it can't break anything, but overriding connect_url enables solving the same "How should things connect to me" problem in multiple places, not just the logs. I'm genuinely not sure what the right answer is.

Another option is to add a separate 'remote_url' for connections from 'remote' hosts (e.g. the host system in the docker case), and store and display this alongside connection_url, essentially baking in the fact that there can never be only one answer to the URL question when running in containers.

Question:
We have a number of extensions that do all three of:
- include front-end assets
- add web services
- replace parts of the notebook stack: kernel_spec_manager_class, etc.

We're looking pretty good on the first two, which will bring much sanity, but the situation is not so rosy for the last.

We accomplish this variously, either by configuring at `enable` time:
- https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/nb_conda_kernels/blob/master/nb_conda_kernels/install.py#L60
Or by patching at runtime:
- https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/nb_conda/blob/master/nb_conda/nbextension/handlers.py#L203

The difference being on when it is reasonable to have manipulated the state of the application.

We'll still have to maintain an explicit configure step to handle these config changes, which will result in a lot of duplicated effort. Also, I don't really like the runtime patching approach.

What would be the fall-out of extending the server extension [spec](https://github.com/ellisonbg/notebook/pull/10/files#diff-a8dbeef5c260c59149ae8f09b88b4dacR299) to enable an update to the config?
So:
```json
{
  "module": "my_extension",
  "config": {
    "NotebookApp": {
      "kernel_spec_manager_class": "my_extension.MyKernelManager"
    }
  } 
}
```

On `enable`, this would set the value. On `disable`, it would unset the values. All the path stuff (`--user`, `--sys-prefix`, etc.) are already known. Is there logic already for doing that kind of un-configuration, or are we stuck rewriting this each time until we have the `conf.d` bag-o'-files?

But to clarify: no, it wasn't a doc build, it was trying to run the notebook itself from a git master checkout.  But I forgot to run `npm run build`, so my JS assets were out of date.  And, not having stated either in my config or at the command line the `ignore_minified_js=True` flag, then I was getting stale JS assets.

The behavior was thus rather confusing: at the command-line, a `jupyter notebook --version` call *looked* OK, since it reported a current version, which was indeed true. But that current version was 'half-current', if you will, since it was 'python-current', but 'javascript-old', as it was still running old JS code, from whenever I had last run a JS build.

So, the summary is, you either:

- get religious about *always* running `npm run build`

- or you configure your setup with the `ignore_minified_js=True` flag.

Since I'm forgetful and I also find the npm build slow, I went for the latter...

First and foremost, I don't want to give the impression that schematics isn't a wonderful piece of engineering.  I use it every day and it does what it was designed to do... quite well.

That said, Python 3 has brought with it quite a few changes.  Some of the data I've encountered legitimately has cycles.  Other data has pretty unusual requirements around correctness (i.e. a dict with a key that could be an int, a string, or another dict with a certain schema).  Finally, there are some dark corners of the codebase where we "do the right thing" that can sometimes manifest as "behavior" (Random references:  #53 + #54 + #76 [semi-intentional sloppy coercion across all of our types], #167 + #184 + #185 + #186 [polymorphic data structures are kind of a pain], #194 [bytes makes this complicated again], #210 [no easy way to validate a whole model without piggy-backing on a field or type validator], #266 + #316 + #318 [issues right at the heart of composition / strictness]).

There's a lot to be done that can adapt to the above issues.  Off the top of my head:

* Make coercion more controllable (i.e. right now, our models have optional strict interpretation but our fields are always loose)
* Make StringFields encoding-smart (i.e. encoding=None [which passes through a string] versus encoding=utf_8/ascii/utf_16/shift_jis/anything_in_codecs/rot13_lol).
* Make ByteFields for when we actually want untouched byte strings.
* Perhaps a relation API to sew together different datasets that have references.
* Find a way to solidly address the concerns of polymorphic data (this is easier when encoding than when decoding)
* Global validators on models.
* Less magic.  We're have a lot of complexity for what we get out of things like meta-classes, I think.
* Perhaps trade our transform loops for some recursion?  I can't help but think that the polymorphism stuff would be easier if we could delegate to the models for some of the composition.

There's probably plenty more, but the overall difference I'm shooting for is:

* take a step back and see if we can refactor the core to better tackle the things it makes difficult
* fit better into Python 3 (the bytes / strings thing is new, very touchy, and encoding is our concern)
* make schematics easier to control when we're strict and when we're sloppy
* make schematics easier to extend (this is basically black-magic beyond simple fields)
* generate more / better ways to make comprehensive validations
* easier to make sophisticated compositions of different schematics in a single data-structure
* easier to know what's going on under the hood--especially with ModelTypes and the like

Anyways, that's just my 0.02¢.  I'd imagine that this is going to take some concrete proposals before we get anywhere.  I'm curious what others have to say.

This is probably a problem that essentially all automatic differentiation algorithm will encounter.

A minimum example looks like this (run it twice to get rid of the warning :-)):

```python
from autograd import grad
import autograd.numpy as np

func = lambda x: np.sqrt(x**2)**2
x = np.array([0.0])
grad_fval = grad(func)(x)
print(grad_fval)
```

Mathematically, the reason is clear when write down the chain rule:

$ d/dt sqrt(x^2)^2 = (2 \* w2) \* (1/2 / w1^1/2) \* (2 \* x), w1 = x^2, w2 = sqrt(x^2). $

When x = 0, it is something like 0 * (1/0) * 0, a typical indeterminate form.

In other words, the function norm() creates a discontinuity of derivative at the origin, but then you square it, making it continued again. The program is just not clever enough to eliminate this spurious discontinuity.

Luckily, as @jermwatt pointed out, there is a warning, and this warning should **not** be ignored. The message `expand(g / ans) * x` essentially `g / ans * x` is the derivative of the L2-norm times `g`.

BTW: I'm worrying about this "bug", because that the highly nonlinear objective function might -- if unfortunate enough -- hit this problem. Do we have a guarantee that the Warning about non-smooth or indeterminate result is always shown?

This is an interesting idea. But it does invite the question: what's so special about `__getitem__`? In Autograd, all functions are pure and all nodes are immutable, so why not memoize everything? Maybe we should? Or is there a reason that `__getitem__` is different? Perhaps we expect it to be called particularly frequently? Or is it easier to memoize than other functions because its arguments can be more easily hashed and compared for equality/identity?

When we know we're going to use a value several times, we usually just store it in a variable:

```python
def f(tup):
    element = tup[0]
    x = np.array([element for _ in xrange(100)])
    return np.sum(x)
```

Do you have a case where doing this would be awkward?

I didn't realize slices couldn't be hashed. That seems quite silly. It looks like the idea was [proposed a few years ago and rejected by Guido](https://bugs.python.org/issue1733184) mostly because slices can be created with special syntax which could lead to confusion: "x[:] should mean slicing, not getitem."

Right, so let's say your charm has two different "modes," selected by a config option that each depend on a different set of other config options.  Your config-changed hook handler could then set one of two "mode states" based on the selector option.  You could even have the hook handler do validation on the additional options and remove the mode state if its dependent options are invalid.  Then, you'd have separate state handlers for each of those two states that would be guaranteed to see the expected settings because they would only trigger if the hook handler had first confirmed which should be set and that it had the additional settings it needed.

The root cause of this is juju/charm-tools#381 which is what causes both versions to end up in the wheelhouse directory in the built charm.  A workaround until that is fixed would be to manually remove the incorrect version from the wheelhouse directory of the built charm.

Since all reactive events occur within the context of a hook and the state of the juju db is snapshotted at each run (realtion settings, configuration, etc) it shouldn't matter when or where during the event train code is run because it will all always run with the same data for the life of that hook event. ```python
```python
            try:
                # Check the response code for error conditions
                check_response_code(response, erc)

                # Catch rate-limit errors
                # Wait and retry if automatic rate-limit handling is enabled
                if self.wait_on_rate_limit and e.retry_after:
                    warnings.warn(SparkRateLimitWarning(response))
                    time.sleep(e.retry_after)
                    continue
                else:
                    # Re-raise the SparkRateLimitError
                    raise
```

Updated title to be a little clearer. The BRs require that names go in the subjectAlternativeName field, regardless of how many of them there are. The Subject CN field is deprecated. So really this is about how many names the official Let's Encrypt client would request in each cert by default.

I don't have the data handy, but there is significant overlap between non-SNI browsers and non-SHA256 browsers. It's not 100%. There are some non-SNI browsers that do support SHA256. We should find out which ones. Any browser without SHA256 is immediately off our supported list, so we don't have to worry about those.

There's an additional advantage to getting single-identifier certs by default: reduced handshake size, meaning faster connection times.

Two other notes:
 - Subscribers are of course free to request multi-identifier certs, but we may not support them in the auto-renewer of the official client on day 1.
 - This is a decision that we can change down the road. I.e. if we default to single-identifier at launch, we can change the default in subsequent client releases. To me, this argues for launching with single identifier by default since it means we can do something simple and robust, reducing the risk to our launch readiness.

Yes, our code only rely on the equality of the same object instance. Our app use Command Pattern to delegate the message processing logic to different commands and consolidate the processed messages using set union operation, something like the following code:
```python
class Command(Interface):
	"""Execute the command on selected messages, return the list of processed messages"""
	def execute(self, messages):
		pass


class Invoker:
	# commands initialize in constructor...
	_commands = []

	def process(self, messages):
		processed_messages = set()
		for command in self._commands:
			processed_messages |= set(command.execute(messages))
		# do work on processed_messages...
```

```python
def get_percentile(personality_traits, trait_name):
```

I have two ideas:

You can get the reply normally, and then check the [`last_match()`](https://rivescript.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rivescript.html#rivescript.rivescript.RiveScript.last_match) function to see if they had matched the `*` trigger. This should probably be okay if your bot doesn't change any user variables in the `*` trigger.

```python
reply = bot.reply(username, message)
if bot.last_match(username) != '*':
    send_reply(username, reply)
else:
    # do something else here
```

Or, if your bot might change user variables and you want to avoid the side effects of variables getting changed, you can do a "test flight" reply using the [`freeze_uservars()`](https://rivescript.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rivescript.html#rivescript.rivescript.RiveScript.freeze_uservars) and [`thaw_uservars()`](https://rivescript.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rivescript.html#rivescript.rivescript.RiveScript.thaw_uservars)

```python
# Snapshot the user's variables
bot.freeze_uservars(username)

# Test the reply
reply = bot.reply(username, message)
is_wildcard = bot.last_match(username) == '*'

# Roll the user vars back so it's as if the reply() never happened.
bot.thaw_uservars(username)

# now you have is_wildcard bool
if is_wildcard:
    do_something_else()
else:
    reply = bot.reply(username, message)  # for real this time
    send_reply(username, reply)
```

I have now completed the initial work and implemented the specialization for vector as well ([see this translation to the previous vector class template](https://github.com/danielschemmel/z3/blob/buffer/src/util/vector.h)). At the moment, this is a fairly conservative reimplementation:
- The new class template behaves like `vector` when its `INITIAL_SIZE` is `0` and like `buffer` otherwise
  - The basic algorithms are all kept (both that of `buffer` and of `vector`)
  - Performance should be at least similar to that before - but only by construction, I have not actually benchmarked this...
- There is a shim in place to enable as both `buffer` and `vector`, which requires one change in the rest of the project: There is [a single instance](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c390b72e42222312ca3492756e083a52cfda3c5f/src/util/lp/dense_matrix_instances.cpp#L38) where the class keyword is used in front of `vector`, which needs to be omitted
- No changes are necessary anywhere else
- I did some very basic testing by compiling everything and pushing a few queries into the resulting z3 binary

To anybody who trained models with any of the old batch normalization implementations, note that the layer stores `inv_std` instead of `var` now.
To convert old models, you will need to update their `inv_std` parameters after `set_all_param_values()`:
```python
set_all_param_values(network, my_loaded_values)
# convert from old batchnorm implementation to new one:
for layer in get_all_layers(network):
    if hasattr(layer, 'inv_std'):
        var = layer.inv_std.get_value()
        inv_std = (1 / np.sqrt(var + layer.epsilon)).astype(var.dtype)
        layer.inv_std.set_value(inv_std)
```

If this turns out to affect a lot of users and a lot of existing models, we can still add a flag to the `BatchNormLayer` that allows to switch back to the old behaviour of computing and storing the variance instead of the inverted standard deviation.

The autogenerated class *Grouper* is not making the *Elems* children (i.e. not setting parent), hence the OrphanContainerWarning. When the parent of a child (in our case None) does not match the container holding reference to said child, it is assumed to be an external child, so a link is set. In this case, a link is made to something that doesn't exist. The *Elem* instance (i.e. `elem`) doesn't exist because it doesn't get visited again during the recursive building of containers. You don't see any attributes on `elem` because it's a broken soft-link.

The correct debugging technique for issues like this is the following:

- turn on simulation manager logging. `logging.getLogger('angr.sim_manager').setLevel('INFO')
- watch the output. notice anything that seems questionable. in this case the presence of errored states is sketchy and there is also a visible path explosion.
- run your program and get a debug shell somewhere near the problematic point. for state-explosion problems, my favorite way to do this is `ipython --pdb test_script.py` and hit ctrl-c when things get hairy
- investigate the content of the simulation manager. here, I looked at the list of errored states and found that all of them had errored with `Unimplemented format specifier 'i'`, indicating that part of the problem is just that angr doesn't support `printf("%i")` yet. You can report this to us or you can fix it yourself. I've fixed this in the current master.
- try again after that bug has been fixed. this time you should notice that you just have a huge state explosion problem.

If you must submit an issue, please include more diagnostic information than "it doesn't work", for example "the script never finished and ate all my memory".

I wasn't able to get your script to work in the end. You have too nasty of a state explosion problem. I tried enabling veritesting but there seems to be a bug in it. One thing you could do would be to add an `avoid` condition to your explore, either blacklisting all the parts where response is set to nonzero, or actively introspecting the stack frame and extracting the value of `response`, testing if it's nonzero, and avoiding the state if it is.

Here's a better version of your script, since I noticed you were a little confused about how to use simfiles. The critical part is that you can't use the original simfile for concretization, since it's attached to the original state, not the found state. You can either concretize the input BVS against the found state (since variables are independent of states but files are not), or you can get the file from the found state and concretize that.

```python
import angr
import claripy
```

> is that because it merged the two paths(blue line in CFG) as one?

Yes, that is exactly why it shows that string, and that is exactly the point of veritesting! Veritesting as a technique is meant to reduce state branching by identifying merge points and performing smart path merging. There is no reasonable string representation of exactly what veritesting does that we could put in the trace, since veritesting can operate on dozens of paths at once, merging paths that diverge wildly but reconvene at a merge point. Trying to stuff all that data in a string would be a nightmare!

You can read the original paper about veritesting [here](https://users.ece.cmu.edu/~dbrumley/pdf/Avgerinos%20et%20al._2014_Enhancing%20Symbolic%20Execution%20with%20Veritesting.pdf).

Isn't it because your query now returns the deleted tombstones `UNION` the deleted timestamps and returns the rowcount as the number of deleted things?

To be honest, I don't think this fixes the problem and I am no longer sure it's worth fixing. I'm not as experienced with SQL stuff as you are but the [Postgres documentation](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/transaction-iso.html) says "UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT FOR UPDATE, and SELECT FOR SHARE commands behave the same as SELECT in terms of searching for target rows: they will only find target rows that were committed as of the command start time." My interpretation of this is that if you have two DELETE queries as subqueries of a single SELECT, things can be committed after the first one and before the second one, which is the thing we wanted to avoid.

I think we should probably just go back to the way the code was before this last commit and (as a follow-up) make the isolation level a Kinto-wide configuration option. Otherwise we're just trying to force transactional semantics by wedging our queries together.

Picking this up from the initial report from @obviousjim.

The repro on this problem involves us running our own capture application with a Realsense D415 sensor (although could be happening on D435 too), waiting until the Realsense SDK locks up or crashes then seeing the result when starting the viewer app without unplugging and plugging the sensor back in. We also see the resulting problem (mismatched stride, discolorization of incoming frames) in our own capture app but confirmed it happens in the viewer app also, indicating the sensor must be in a bad state from the previous crash or lockup (from my POV).

We are seeing this most prevalently whilst testing on a Macbook Air with sdk v2.13 in our App and the viewer and latest matching sensor firmware v5.9.14.

This does not happen on every crash of the SDK, so is not reproducable every run but is a critical bug for our product.

The Realsense viewer version is v2.13.0 inline with the SDK version and freshly pulled and compiled from master.

Thanks!

Neil

When Lori and I were discussing ways to reorganize the directory structure, I brought up this pmag.py and ipmag.py merge possibility. Lori's perspective (which she convinced me of at the time) is that it makes sense to keep them separate with pmag.py containing the functions that mostly do a single thing and ipmag.py containing functions that bring pmag.py functions together to do more of a work flow (e.g. do a bootstrap reversal test and have options to save the associated output). In this way, the ipmag.py functions are similar to the command line programs that are built on top of pmag.py functions, but are built to be used without sys.argv calls, etc. The current split between the modules isn't entirely consistent with this design conception, but it largely is. Worth talking about more moving forward...

In order to make the ``ppaged()`` method works as intended, we had to be very careful about what pager we used and what flags were sent to it.  

Theoretically it is nice to allow a user to choose which pager they want to use with the **PAGER** environment variable.  However, this also gives end users to effectively break ``cmd2`` applications without knowing why.  

I think I would want something like a boolean variable in the ``cmd2.Cmd`` class which would allow ``cmd2`` applications to explicitly bypass using the **PAGER** environment variable to ensure consistent behavior.  Alternatively we could make the underlying command called by ``ppaged`` is a runtime settable parameter akin to the editor.

Minor style comment: It might be good to agree on a docstring style for consistency. In C++:
```c++
/** documentation
 * string
 */
```
vs.
```c++
/** documentation
    string */
```

In Python:
```python
# Documentation string
def foo():
    pass
```
vs.
```python
def foo():
    """Documentation string"""
```

In Python, I'd strongly recommend using the standard triple-quote docstrings. In C++, I don't think it matters as long as it's consistent (although the asterisk-less version has better sphinx compatibility).

Hi @fideloper, this is the intended behavior. Each invocation of `certbot` only ever obtains a single certificate. The certificate you obtained, however, will contain subjectAltName extensions so that it applies to all of the domains you mentioned.

When the documentation says "When requesting a certificate for multiple domains, each domain will use the most recently specified --webroot-path`", it really means requesting "a" certificate for many domains, as there is no way to do the contrary. Can you think of a way that the documentation would have been clearer to you?

I think we can stop adding the name of the package to the plugin flag and just use the name of the entry point which the plugin author controls. The big thing here is we don't want to break backwards compatibility. The `package_name:entry_point_name` version of the flags still need to work. This affects both adding the plugin flags and fetching  the values from the parsed config.

Perhaps a simple solution is to pass multiple flags to `add_argument` [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/plugins/common.py#L79) if `option_namespace` contains the package name. The `option_namespace` should probably still comes first as that's what affects the path in the parsed config, however, plugins can access these values using [conf()](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/plugins/common.py#L103) so they don't have to see this ugliness. Only downside with this approach I see is the `package_name:entry_point_name` version will be used in renewal configuration files, but I can't see any other problems here. I definitely could be overlooking something though so this will require some experimentation and testing.

Here are some thoughts from my side: processing many files with scour is "slow", because of 2 reasons:

a) Python startup time and
b) serial execution

a) is actually quite good with CPython (we should probably note in the docs that running Scour on PyPy works, but doesn't make sense), and it is not something we can do a lot about - other than processing multiple files in one run.

however, I am not sure if it is worth, because b) can be solved (and often is) using `make -jN`.

IMO, we should first look at how Scour is actually used in context. Eg if I am using it as part of a build process, then the build tool I use should be able to parallelize invocations of Scour. Eg if used from within Inkscape, as an exporter, @Ede123 will know best, but with such interactive use, I wouldn't expect a Scour internal parallelization to be important.

So actually, I am not convinced of the _why_, and for what goal / in what context ..

A nasty thing came up in the latest commit. There are **set** commands in the instrument API that take multiple input arguments. Furthermore, **none** of the corresponding parameters can be read back out. What is a good solution for that?

What I came up with is the following. Let's say params A, B, and C are set in the same API call. When setting A, we then call `get_latest` on B and C and add that to the command going to the instrument. Now, upon initialisation, we don't know what B and C are, since they can't be queried. We therefore assign **arbitrary** initial values to them. This means that right after init, setting A will have the side-effect of setting B and C as well. So we pray that our default values were "good".

Is there a better way to do this?

The error happens when 2 threads are calling `get_object` simultaneously, and both need object reconstruction. 

More detailed process:
1. `get_object` will first try to fetch the objects, if any objects are missing, it asks node manager for reconstruction;
2. When node manager receives the request, it marks the worker as blocked, and recycles its resources.
3. When all objects become available, worker notifies node-manager that it's unblocked now, and NM gives back its resources.

Then, when more than 1 thread are hitting step 2 and 3, there's a race condition.

=====

The simplest way to work around this is to just lock step 2 and step 3. It's not ideal, but should be okay because reconstruction isn't very common and perf in single thread shouldn't be hurt. 

To really address this issue in multi-threading scenario, we need a way to know if all threads in the worker are blocked. That will become much more complex.

@robertnishihara, I'm thinking of addressing this issue by locking the reconstruction part. Do you have any suggestions? Thank you!

@robertnishihara, @pcmoritz  Thanks for the clarifications and I apologize if my replies yesterday were a bit vague. I started making sense of the stack and did some more experiments today. Here are my observations.

1. In all the previous experiments the plasma object store was the one causing the swap writes. Based on https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/1175#issuecomment-341206695 I'd say that was because the default is set too high considering the ring buffer takes 10GB.

2. I configured the script with `ray.init(object_store_memory=1000000000)` on the 16GB machine and 4GB on the 128GB/40 threads one and things started to behave :). The plasma object store is now using about 60% of the value I specified in the `ray.init()`. **Question:** what is a good heuristic for setting this parameter?

3. With the plasma store size fixed I noticed the `redis` server process was now the one increasing enough to provoke a swap write in a few million steps (for example on the 128GB system the redis process is at 4.3GB after 4.4 million steps). If by not working on multiple machines you mean not working on a cluster -- then that defeats the whole point :)

There are two cases I can see for ray.init:
1) Local cluster -- ray.init() with no args.
2) Actual cluster -- ray.init(redis_addr=<addr>)

In the second case, since <addr> is essentially a random value, basically you can't have scripts that work portably across multiple clusters -- you have to patch the script to point to the node runtime IP.

Actually the ideal API to just always use ray.init(), and allow switching to actual cluster mode via a env var, e.g. `RAY_CLUSTER=localhost script.py`. That way script code never needs to change.

@robertnishihara 
`Ray.put` is also fine here, but may not be the best API for parameters.
Because `Pay.put` looks more like putting the object into the global object store. But here, this `1` doesn't have to be in the global object store, because it can be passed by value.

-----

Here's my proposal about the `RayObject` API:

`RayObject` is just an abstract interface that has a `get` method:
```java
public interface RayObject <T> {
    public T get();
}
```
It has 2 sub-classes: 
1) `RayGlobalObject` represents an object that is in the global object store. It has an UniqueID, and may or may not be locally available.
2) `RayLocalObject` is for objects that will only be needed locally, for example, parameters.

`RayGlobalObject` can be attained via `Ray.put`, `RayLocalObject` can be attained via `Ray.param`. (`Ray.param` is the same as the above `RayObject.of`, but this name should be better because of consistency with `Ray.put`)

Thus, users can use either `Ray.put` or `Ray.param`, depending on whether they want to put the object in global object store or not.

How does this sound to you?

```
import trio

async def call_repeatedly(interval, func, *args, task_status=trio.TASK_STATUS_IGNORED):
    with trio.open_cancel_scope() as scope:
        task_status.started(scope)
        while True:
            await trio.sleep(interval)
            func(*args)

async def main():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        repeater = await nursery.start(call_repeatedly, 1, print, "Hello")
        await trio.sleep(10)
        repeater.cancel()
trio.run(main)
```

Hmm.

My intuition is that the pseudo-task works like a real task as far as the nursery's task handling is concerned, but it doesn't look like a separate task when you look at the code. What's more, all of contextvars, Python scoping, and debugger introspection agree with that.

So document that the inner task is "somewhat" special, but otherwise let's keep things the way they work now. You want something that behaves like a separate task in all respects? start a separate task.

Hey Miguel!

So the example in the tutorial,
```
 - Name: games_played
       Type: integer
       When: source.event_id == 'game_start'
       Value: session_stats.games_played + 1

- Name: games_won
       Type: integer
       When: source.event_id == 'game_end' and source.won == '1'
       Value: session_stats.games_won + 1
```

will turn
```

1 { "user_id": "09C1", "session_id": "915D", "country" : "US", "event_id": "game_start" }
2 { "user_id": "09C1", "session_id": "915D", "country" : "US", "event_id": "game_end", "won": 1 }
3 { "user_id": "09C1", "session_id": "915D", "country" : "US", "event_id": "game_start" }
4 { "user_id": "09C1", "session_id": "915D", "country" : "US", "event_id": "game_end", "won": 1 }

```

into `915D    09C1    2    2`

But what happens if row 3 is missing? I'm assuming that games_played will stay 1 and games_won will be 2.  The user might instead want to remove the session from the results or have an error message. Note that in this case we can see that the result is wrong in its end state, so we could catch it by doing a check after processing. But sometimes it can only be detected while processing, e.g. if rows 1 and 4 are missing.

Implementation-wise, my first thought was to be able to define something like:

```
- Name: games_won
       Type: integer
       When: source.event_id == 'game_end' and source.won == '1'
       Value: session_stats.games_won + 1
       AssertBefore: session_stats.games_played > session_stats.games_won
--or
       AssertAfter: session_stats.games_played >= session_stats.games_won
       IfError: ignore session_id
```

But it can get a bit messy. Then I thought, an assert is pretty much the same as `When`, so we could add a field like this:

```
- Name: cannot_win_more_than_played
       Type: integer
       When: session_stats.games_played > session_stats.games_won
       Value: session_stats.cannot_win_more_than_played + 1
```

or perhaps, to avoid have lots of validation fields:
```
- Name: game_validation
       Type: error_map
       When1: session_stats.games_played > session_stats.games_won
       Value1: "fewer games_played than games_won"

       When2: ...
       Value2: ""
```
the error_map type should evaluate to true if there were any errors and have the count of the different errors as entries but it changes the general structure.

If the user can rely on the order of field declaration for the order of evaluation, the second form of validations doesn't need any changes; otherwise, there needs to be a way to know whether a validation runs before or after the event is read (could be that all validations are always run afterwards).

Hope it makes sense!

The constraint I want us to be sensitive to is this: there should be only one obvious way to do any given task. I understand the desire for more knobs, e.g., a way of serializing to one file, or multiple files, or a tarball, or to network (all of which might be the most appropriate in some context), but it makes things more confusing for users and implementors. (cough cough TensorFlow three saved model formats cough). Similarly, if we put in an extension point and let everyone do what they want, you lose the benefits of having a standard way of loading protobufs. It's OK to leave ourselves extension points (e.g., defining the tensor data identifier to be a URL seems like a good idea), but until there is a pressing need for fancy things, the standard should specify that there is only one valid form for the URI: a single filename, no scheme, no domain, no directories. There's also security concerns; models should not be allowed to declare that tensors should be loaded from arbitrary paths on a users system.

```python
# coding=utf8

from contextlib import contextmanager

class Config:
    def __init__(self, jupyter_image_type):
        self.jupyter_image_type = jupyter_image_type
c = Config('svg')

@contextmanager
def contexts(jupyter_image_type):
    pr_type = c.jupyter_image_type
    try:
        c.jupyter_image_type = jupyter_image_type
        yield
    finally:
        c.jupyter_image_type = pr_type

def do():
    print('Do with {}'.format(c.jupyter_image_type))


# Usage

print(c.jupyter_image_type)
with contexts('png'):
    do()
print(c.jupyter_image_type)
```

```py
    @spy_ldap('search_s')
    def test_dn_not_cached(self, mock):
        self._init_settings(
            USER_SEARCH=LDAPSearch("ou=people,o=test", ldap.SCOPE_SUBTREE, '(uid=%(user)s)'),
        )
        for _ in range(2):
            user = authenticate(username='alice', password='password')
            self.assertIsNotNone(user)
        # Should have executed once per auth.
        self.assertEqual(mock.call_count, 2)
        # DN is not cached.
        self.assertIsNone(cache.get('django_auth_ldap.user_dn.alice'))

    @spy_ldap('search_s')
    def test_dn_cached(self, mock):
        self._init_settings(
            USER_SEARCH=LDAPSearch("ou=people,o=test", ldap.SCOPE_SUBTREE, '(uid=%(user)s)'),
            CACHE_TIMEOUT=60,
        )
        for _ in range(2):
            user = authenticate(username='alice', password='password')
            self.assertIsNotNone(user)
        # Should have executed only once.
        self.assertEqual(mock.call_count, 1)
        # DN is cached.
        self.assertEqual(
            cache.get('django_auth_ldap.user_dn.alice'),
            'uid=alice,ou=people,o=test',
        )

    def test_deprecated_cache_groups(self):
        self._init_settings(
            USER_SEARCH=LDAPSearch("ou=people,o=test", ldap.SCOPE_SUBTREE, '(uid=%(user)s)'),
            CACHE_GROUPS=True,
        )
        with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w:
            warnings.simplefilter('always')
            user = authenticate(username='alice', password='password')
        self.assertIsNotNone(user)
        self.assertEqual(len(w), 1)
        self.assertEqual(w[0].category, DeprecationWarning)
        self.assertEqual(
            str(w[0].message),
            'Using deprecated settings AUTH_LDAP_CACHE_GROUPS and '
            'AUTH_LDAP_GROUP_CACHE_TIMEOUT, convert to '
            'AUTH_LDAP_CACHE_TIMEOUT instead.',
        )
        # DN is cached.
        self.assertEqual(
            cache.get('django_auth_ldap.user_dn.alice'),
            'uid=alice,ou=people,o=test',
        )
```
